candidate_problems.text: What is the precise boundary between logical/design-space orthogonality and equilibrium-space non-orthogonality? The conjecture asserts a split, but leaves open a formal criterion for when repeated interaction actually constrains viable intelligence-goal pairings rather than merely changing optimal policies. A key problem is to characterize the environmental parameters—observability, memory, identity persistence, coalition formation, enforcement capacity, horizon length, and agent heterogeneity—under which selection acts on terminal goals themselves versus only on strategic expression.

Do repeated-interaction pressures genuinely reshape terminal goals over time, or do they only select for instrumentally cooperative policies masking arbitrary underlying ends? This is a central unresolved tension because the conjecture sometimes speaks as if certain goals are less sustainable, but its own mechanism may support only policy convergence, not goal convergence. The new problem is to determine whether ecological/evolutionary dynamics can discriminate among hidden final goals when behavior is strategically legible and cooperative on the surface.

How robust is the conjecture once agents become highly capable at altering the game structure itself? Very intelligent agents may reduce iterability, hide defections, manipulate reputation systems, fragment identities, seize enforcement mechanisms, or build unilateral advantage that escapes reciprocity pressures. This raises the open question of whether repeated interaction is a stable structural filter or only a contingent regime that advanced agents can dissolve. The problem is to model endogenously changing institutions and power asymmetries rather than treating the iterated environment as fixed.
